items.q: How did the feudal system work in Norman England?
items.correct: The king granted land to tenants-in-chief in return for knight service; they in turn enfeoffed knights who held land in return for military service to their lord.
items.misconception: Feudalism meant peasants were slaves owned by the king.
items.random_wrong: Feudalism was a written constitution introduced by William in 1066.
items.why: Feudalism described a tenurial chain of obligations among free landholders, not slavery. Most peasants were villeins tied to manors — a related but separate system.

items.q: How did Malcolm X's approach differ from MLK's?
items.correct: MLK preached nonviolent integration; Malcolm X (until 1964) advocated black self-defence, separatism, and rejected integration as a goal — though both shifted positions over time.
items.misconception: Malcolm X advocated violent terrorism against white Americans.
items.random_wrong: Malcolm X and MLK were close allies who jointly led the March on Washington.
items.why: Malcolm X advocated self-defence, not aggressive terrorism. Students contrast them too sharply; both critiqued American racism, with Malcolm X moderating after his 1964 Hajj.

items.q: When did Nazi Germany move to a total war economy?
items.correct: Only after Stalingrad in 1943, under Speer; before that Hitler had avoided full mobilisation to maintain civilian morale and consumer goods.
items.misconception: Germany was on a total war footing from 1939 onwards.
items.random_wrong: Germany never moved to a war economy because Hitler thought he would win quickly.
items.why: Germany famously delayed full mobilisation, expecting short wars. Students assume dictatorship meant immediate total war; in fact Hitler feared domestic unrest.
Correct answer: A famous slave ship. “A plantation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Who watched and punished enslaved workers?
items.why: It's Overseers. “Merchants” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what fraction of captives died on the Middle Passage (1?)?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
